g.data-narration: Welcome to statistical inference for large samples. We will explore comparing two data groups using the Z-test.
In engineering, we compare algorithms, products, and systems. Is the new version truly better than the old one?
The Central Limit Theorem states that sample means form a normal distribution regardless of the population shape.
g.text: For large samples (n > 30), the sampling distribution of the sample mean (x̄) is

g.data-narration: Every test starts with two hypotheses: the null hypothesis assumes no difference, while the alternative is what we want to prove.
The Z-test compares two large independent samples. The formula calculates how many standard errors apart the means are.
g.text: The test statistic measures the difference between sample means in units of standard error:

g.data-narration: The standard error accounts for variability within each sample and sample sizes. Larger samples reduce the standard error.
g.text: For two independent samples, the standard error is calculated as:

g.data-narration: Combining the formulas, we get the complete Z-statistic. This tells us how far our observed difference is from zero.
Using the critical value approach, we compare our calculated Z to the Z-table. If it falls in the rejection region, we reject H₀.
The p-value approach calculates the probability of observing our data if the null hypothesis were true. Lower p-value means stronger evidence.
g.text: P-value: Probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as observed, assuming H₀ is true.

g.data-narration: Let's apply this to a real example. A researcher tests if a new compression algorithm is faster than the standard one.
g.text: New algorithm (Group B) vs Standard (Group A). Each group has n = 40 files.

g.data-narration: Calculating the Z-statistic: We plug in our sample values. The result is -2.024, which tells us the new algorithm is faster.
Since -2.024 is less than -1.645 (critical value for one-tailed test), we reject the null hypothesis. The new algorithm is faster.
g.text: Conclusion: Reject H₀. The new compression algorithm is significantly faster.

g.data-narration: Let's summarize: We use the two-sample Z-test for large independent samples to determine if differences are statistically significant.
Remember the formula and decision rules. Reject H₀ if calculated Z exceeds the critical value or if p-value is less than alpha.
g.text: Reject H₀ if: |Z_calc| > Critical Z OR p-value < α

g.data-narration: This technique is essential for A/B testing, algorithm comparison, and quality control. Practice with real-world data to master it.
g.text: • A/B testing for websites and apps
